data.title: Malaysia's 1Q GDP Growth Expected to Beat Advance Estimates, Kenanga SaysMarket Talk
data.description: Malaysia's 1Q GDP growth is expected to reach 4.9%, beating the advance estimate of 4.4%, driven by strength in services and manufacturing, Kenanga economists say in a note. While Trump's tariff threats may pressure exports, domestic-oriented sectors are supported by robust domestic spending, an investment upcycle and rising household income, they say. Malaysia may also benefit from trade and investment shifts as the U.S. continues to decouple from China, they say. Kenanga maintains its 2025 GDP forecast at 4.8%, with 2Q likely to benefit from frontloading ahead of tariff risks. However, a prolonged trade conflict may prompt growth estimates to be revised by 0.3-0.5 percentage points, Kenanga adds. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Meta Bright to Install, Manage Solar PV Systems for Two Best Fresh Mart's Retail Outlets
data.description:
Meta Bright Group , via its unit FBO Land (Serendah), bagged a deal to install and manage solar PV systems at two Best Fresh Mart retail outlets under a zero capital expenditure (capex) model.

The solar PV systems will be operated at the supermarket chain's locations in Malaysian states Selangor and Melaka, according to a Thursday Malaysian bourse filing.

The installations are pending final approval from Tenaga Nasional, the Sustainable Energy Development Authority, and the Energy Commission.

data.title: Polypropylene (PP injection) spot prices down adjust in Europe
data.description: This week, PP injection spot prices slipped, while PP spot Block Copoilymer and Contract remained steady in the European region.An industry source in Europe informed a Polymerupdate team member, "The European polypropylene market experienced a decline in prices for injection-grade polypropylene this week, driven by ongoing weak demand and limited market activity. The presence of abundant, lower-cost imports also contributed to downward pressure on prices. Spot prices for polypropylene continued to drop as numerous sellers adjusted their pricing strategies downward. This situation led to significant fluctuations in upstream crude oil and naphtha prices, causing many market participants to adopt a cautious approach. Contrary to earlier expectations that trading activity would increase in April compared to March, the European PP market remained subdued. March had already seen reduced buying activity due to the anticipation that polypropylene prices would decrease in line with falling propylene feedstock prices. The weakening demand for polypropylene is being felt across various sectors, particularly in the automotive industry, where there is low interest in major purchases, as well as in construction and rigid applications. The economic outlook has shifted to indicate a higher likelihood of recession this year, fueled by increased uncertainty surrounding US tariffs. This has undermined confidence among participants in the polypropylene market, leading many to avoid risks associated with long-haul imports from the Middle East and Asia Pacific. In addition to the pricing risks linked to fluctuations in upstream feedstock costs, the combination of low demand and abundant supply in the European market is further discouraging activity.The source added, "This week, spot material was reported to be readily available, with imported supplies from Asia also on the market. The decline in freight rates from Asia to Europe, coupled with a weakening dollar, has enhanced arbitrage opportunities for South Korean sellers. Additionally, material from the Middle East was noted to be accessible. A significant escalation in the trade conflict between the US and China may impact European polypropylene (PP) pricing dynamics. China's imposition of high tariffs on US imports, including propane, has increased the baseline for PP production costs within China. This situation is anticipated to lead to a decrease in the operating rates of Chinese PP plants, potentially allowing sellers from other Asian nations to concentrate more on supplying the Chinese market. While this could provide some relief for European producers, it remains uncertain whether there will be any shifts in PP demand in China and other Asian markets in the future. Buyers are reportedly delaying their purchases due to a downward trend in upstream naphtha prices, leading to expectations of a reduction in the May propylene market clearing price (MCP). Market participants are also closely monitoring how tariffs may influence trade flows for polypropylene (PP) and its feedstock. The negative outlook for feedstock has prompted a pullback in demand, causing buyers to exercise increased caution amid predictions of falling PP prices in the near future. Many buyers are postponing their procurement until May when feasible, opting to purchase only as necessary. The ability of buyers to do this largely depends on their existing stock levels, particularly since some had previously reduced their procurement in early March due to a similar pricing forecast for the upcoming month. Additionally, market players have noted weaker-than-anticipated demand across various value chains, which has diminished converters' urgency to replenish their stocks, especially with the upcoming Easter holiday closures at many plants. However, many buyers will likely need to restock in May as their inventories dwindle. This situation, combined with a decrease in imports, may provide some leverage to PP producers in maintaining contract margins, although it is premature to draw definitive conclusions. In April, PP prices have continued to trend downward. Ongoing negotiations for PP contracts indicate that producers are aiming to expand their margins by retaining part of the Euro 55/mt reduction in the April MCP for feedstock propylene. There have been reports of earlier price offers being lowered, but these have not materialized as buyers are advocating for more significant reductions. Commodity grades of PP homopolymer injection and PP block copolymer are reportedly facing greater pressure, while specialized PP grades, except those linked to the automotive sector, may be better positioned to recover some margin or at least avoid margin erosion. Significant price reductions could occur in instances where base prices are elevated or where producers are under pressure to sell specific grades.In the spot markets, PP injection moulding grade prices were assessed at the Euro 1095-1105/mt FD North West Europe mark, a week on week drop of Euro (-20/mt). Meanwhile, PP block copolymer grade prices were assessed at the Euro 1175-1185/mt FD Northwest Europe levels, steady from the previous week.Meanwhile in the contract markets, PP injection moulding grade prices were assessed at the Euro 1520-1525/mt FD NWE Germany and FD NWE France levels, both steady from the previous week. PP injection moulding grade prices were assessed at the Euro 1510-1515/mt FD NWE Italy levels, unchanged week on week. Meanwhile, PP injection moulding grade prices were assessed at the GBP 1305-1310/mt FD NWE UK levels, a fall of GBP (-10/mt) from last week.In the contract markets, PP block copolymer grade prices were assessed at the Euro 1590-1595/mt FD NWE Germany and FD NWE France levels, both left unchanged week on week. PP block copolymer grade prices were assessed at the Euro 1580-1585/mt FD NWE Italy levels, stable from last week. Meanwhile, PP block copolymer grade prices were assessed at the GBP 1365-1370/mt FD NWE UK levels, flat from the previous week.FCA Antwerp PP homopolymer prices were assessed at the Euro 1080-1110/mt level, a drop of Euro (-20/mt) from last week, while FCA Antwerp PP copolymer prices were assessed at the Euro 1160-1190/mt levels, steady week on week.Upstream propylene spot prices on Thursday were assessed at the Euro 855-865/mt FD Northwest Europe levels, sharply lower by Euro (-55/mt) from last week.European propylene contract price for April 2025 settled at the Euro 1080/MT FD North West Europe levels. This price represents a sharp fall of Euro 55/mt from its March 2025 settlement levels.

data.title: U.S. House Committee Urges Two U.S. Banks to Withdraw From CATL's Listing2nd Update
data.description:
By Sherry Qin

A U.S. congressional committee has urged two U.S. banks to pull out of working on Chinese battery giant Contemporary Amperex Technology's planned listing in Hong Kong, expressing "significant concerns" over their involvement in the process.

By underwriting CATL's initial public offering, Bank of America and JPMorgan Chase are exposing themselves to "serious regulatory, financial, and reputational risks," Rep. John Moolenaar (R., Mich.), chairman of the House Select Committee on the Chinese Communist Party, wrote in letters addressed to the lenders' chief executives on Thursday.

The letters to BofA CEO Brian Moynihan and JPMorgan's Jamie Dimon made reference to President Trump's "America First Investment Policy," aimed at preventing U.S. investments into companies allegedly tied to China's military.

"CATL's IPO is the exact kind of investment this policy was designed to block," wrote Moolenaar, head of the committee that describes itself as committed to building bipartisan consensus on the threat posed by the Chinese Communist Party.

The Pentagon in January added CATL and other well-known Chinese businesses to a list of companies it identifies as military in nature. The battery maker protested the move at the time and a spokesperson reiterated on Friday that the designation of CATL as a Chinese military company is a mistake.

"CATL has never engaged in any military-related business or activities," the company spokesperson said.

The allegations in the letter by Representative Moolenaar are factually incorrect and without merit, the spokesperson said. CATL's Hong Kong listing is progressing as planned and it is engaging with the U.S. Department of Defense to clarify any misunderstandings, the spokesperson added.

Bank of America and JPMorgan Chase declined to comment.

The electric-vehicle battery maker, which is listed in Shenzhen, applied for a secondary listing in Hong Kong earlier this year to advance its global expansion strategy and improve its competitiveness.

Analysts say CATL could raise around $5 billion in the offering, which would make it the city's largest listing since ByteDance rival Kuaishou's $5.4 billion IPO in 2021.

CATL, which supplies batteries to automakers such as Tesla and Volkswagen, accounted for more than one-third of the global battery market in 2024, more than double that of BYD, which ranked second, according to Seoul-based SNE Research.

Shares of CATL ended 0.2% lower on Friday.

Write to Sherry Qin at sherry.qin@wsj.com

data.title: Italy's Annual Construction Output Rises 6% in February
data.description:
Italy's calendar-adjusted construction output index climbed 6% year over year in February, following a 4.2% increase in January, Istat said Friday.

On a monthly basis, Italy's seasonally adjusted construction output grew 0.3%.

data.title: Eastern Pioneer Driving School's Vice Chairman Resigns
data.description:
Eastern Pioneer Driving School's vice chairman, Yan Wenhui, resigned due to work arrangements, according to a Shanghai Stock Exchange disclosure on Thursday.

Yan will continue to serve as a director and general manager of the Chinese automobile driving school company.

The company plans to elect a new vice chairman as soon as possible, the disclosure said.

The company's shares closed 1% higher.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Friday at 5 AM ET
data.description:
Dow Industrials Fall, Capping Another Tough Week on Wall Street

Nvidia stock slips amid trade tensions; Trump renews attacks on Powell.

U.S. Moves Ahead With Plan to Charge Fees on Chinese Ships

The U.S. trade representative plans to charge ships for each voyage, not for each U.S. port call.

Why World Leaders Are Pulling Their Punches in the Trade War

As countries negotiate with the Trump administration, they will have to decide whether to stick with that approach or try using retaliation as leverage.

Trump and Powell on Collision Course Without Easy Escape

The president wants an interest-rate cut, but his trade war has the Fed chair boxed in.

Chinese Premier Urges Officials to Think Outside the Box as Tariffs Bite

Chinese Premier Li Qiang encouraged officials to "dare to break norms," saying the government should act swiftly to shore up market expectations, the state-run Xinhua News Agency reported.

Private Equity World Engulfed by Perfect Storm

Tariff-related market turmoil dashes investors' hopes for payouts as dealmaking grinds to a near standstill.

Japan Inflation Speeds Up, Backing Case for Rate Hikes

Japan's core consumer inflation gained pace in March due to higher food prices, helping the Bank of Japan make a case for further interest-rate increases despite tariff concerns.

Bonds struggle since 'liberation day' tariffs, but Fed is in no rush to cut rates

The U.S. bond market has been in rough patch since President Donald Trump announced "Liberation Day" tariffs.

Rare Earths Show What Trump Gets Right, and Wrong, on Trade

China understands that there are more weapons in a trade war besides tariffs. The White House should consider what that means for U.S. trade policy.

Americans Worry Over Negative Impact of China Tariffs

Respondents in a recent Pew survey see trade as unbalanced, but are divided over the merit of increasing levies.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Friday at 5 AM ET
data.description:
U.S. House Committee Urges Two U.S. Banks to Withdraw From CATL's Listing

A U.S. congressional committee has urged Bank of America and JPMorgan to pull out of working on the Chinese battery giant's planned listing in Hong Kong.

Bill Ackman Teases Prospect of Uber, Hertz Partnership

Billionaire investor discloses nearly 20% stake in Hertz, lays out path to greater profitability

Netflix, Unscathed by Tariffs, Posts Record Profit

The streamer forecasts stronger revenue growth in the second quarter.

Apple Loses Ground in China's Smartphone Market as Local Rivals Gain

Apple lost its top spot in China's smartphone market, dethroned by local rival Xiaomi as Beijing's consumption-boosting subsidies help buoy demand for cheaper products.

The Week Google and Meta Faced an Antitrust Reckoning

The two titans of digital advertising are facing unprecedented legal threats over tactics they used to reach dominance.

It looks like Intel may have to go it alone. Can CEO Lip-Bu Tan fix the company solo?

Another rumored partner has dismissed the prospect of an arrangement with Intel, suggesting the company won't get outside help with its turnaround.

The Chip Industry Has Too Many Eggs in the AI Basket

TSMC struck an upbeat note after posting strong earnings, but tariffs and the trade war loom large.

L'Oreal Sales Rise as Europe Growth Offsets U.S. Weakness

L'Oréal Chief Executive Nicolas Hieronimus told analysts the beauty market is now more likely to grow this year at the lower end of the 4% to 4.5% range he predicted in February.

Judge Rules Google Operates Illegal Ad Monopoly

The tech giant faces multiple legal threats related to how it wields market power.

Hermès Is Hiking Prices. Other Luxury Companies Will Follow.

The luxury company will increase prices starting May 1.

data.title: Samty Residential Raises Forecasts Following Asset Disposal, Boosts Distributions
data.description:
Samty Residential Investment has raised its financial forecasts upward for the six months ending July 31, according to its Thursday bourse filing.

It now projects a 10.9% increase in net income to 2.19 billion yen from 1.98 billion yen initially, and raises distributions per unit by 6.9% to 2,796 yen from 2,616 yen previously, including excess in earnings.

The Reit's operating revenue forecast was raised to 6.15 billion yen from 5.94 billion yen.

The revision follows the disposal of three properties namely S-FORT Kita-Sanno, S-FORT Motohama, and S-FORT Takasaki for 2.43 billion yen, generating a 211 million yen in gains.

In a separate filing, the Reit said the assets were sold due to anticipated rising maintenance costs from ageing, with proceeds to be returned to unitholders and reserved for future investments.

Post-disposal, Samty's portfolio will comprise 188 properties valued at 169.6 billion yen, with 73.6% allocated to regional cities.

data.title: FBN INTERVIEW WITH REP. JEFF VAN DREW (R-NJ)
data.description:
TRANSCRIPT

April 18, 2025

NEWS PROGRAM

REP. JEFF VAN DREW (R-NJ)

FBN INTERVIEW WITH REP. JEFF VAN DREW (R-NJ)

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

20 East Thomas Road, Suite 2200

Phoenix, AZ 85012

asc.info@viqsolutions.com

Copyright 2025. Provided under license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

All materials herein are protected by United States copyright law

and/or license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc., and may not be

reproduced, distributed, transmitted, displayed, published or

broadcast without the prior written permission of

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

You may not alter or remove any trademark, copyright or other

notice from copies of the content.

FBN INTERVIEW WITH REP. JEFF VAN DREW (R-NJ)

APRIL 18, 2025

SPEAKERS:

REP. JEFF VAN DREW (R-NJ)

JACKIE DEANGELIS, FBN HOST

JACKIE DEANGELIS: Edward Lawrence, thank you so much for that great interview.

Joining us now, New Jersey Congressman Jeff Van Drew.

Congressman, always great to have you. We've got President Trump sounding confident that the United States can make a deal with the E.U. and China, as I call it, he drops nuggets. He lets us know something is coming. What do you think?

REP. JEFF VAN DREW (R-NJ): I think we're in good shape. I know it's a little bumpy. I know some people are concerned. I understand all of that, but this is what he does well.

For too long, for too many years, it has been Europe, it has been rest of the world that controls our future, opening up markets to our goods, our products is only fair. You know, after World War Two, we were a very generous country, and we allowed all kinds of terms and conditions and tariffs upon us, but we didn't reciprocate. It's not fair.

And we've seen how much manufacturing and how much production we've lost in the United States of America. We've got to ride through this. We're having good talks with Japan. We had great talks with Mexico. We had great talks with Italy. We're going to do this, we're going to do it, and we're going to be the better for it. We've just got to hold on a little bit.

DEANGELIS: And Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent spoke earlier today on the status of some of the trade negotiations. Let's listen.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

SCOTT BESSENT, U.S. TREASURY SECRETARY: We've had a process in place. We're working on the big 15 economies. First, we had a fantastic meeting with Japan yesterday. I believe there have been calls with the E.U. already, and then we have South Korea coming in next week, and I believe India is also talking — that's moving very quickly.

(END VIDEO CLIP)

DEANGELIS: The million dollar question for America and the markets also Congressman, is if we can expect significant progress in these negotiations before the end of the 90-day pause and then, in effect, you see the art of the deal come together because the strict tariffs that we saw at the beginning on Liberation Day, they never actually go into effect.

VAN DREW: Well. And you know what? This is somebody who knows how to negotiate, President Trump does. And I think you are going to see significant movement in that 90-day envelope of opportunity, in that period, it may not be every single country. We're going to find out, but you know, this is a little bit of playing chicken.

He's going to look them straight in the eye. This is the deal. Do you want to participate? Do you want to make it good for you and us, or do you want to just sit back and have it happen to you?

DEANGELIS: Yes.

VAN DREW: I don't think they want to do that. I think they're going to come to the table. They are coming to the table. It's been remarkable how many countries already have come to the table.

DEANGELIS: And I think if he gets enough of the big countries within the 90-day period, then he can extend his pause potentially as he works out the smaller deals. Meantime, Trump posting on Truth Social this morning that Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell is always too late and too wrong when it comes to interest rate cuts, and that he should have lowered interest rates a long time ago in response to Powell saying that Trump's policies are moving us away from our goals, and now Trump is accusing Powell of playing politics. Let's watch.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

TRUMP: So price is coming down. The only thing that's going up actually is interest rates, because we have a Federal Reserve Chairman that is playing politics, somebody that I've never been very fond of, actually, but he's playing politics.

Interest rates should be down now. They should be coming down. In Europe, as you know, they reduced them, but our guy wants to play —

(END VIDEO CLIP)

DEANGELIS: Congressman, it is interesting to me that Powell was willing to cut interest rates three times before the presidential election, but now all of a sudden, there's a pause in place, and he's not willing to move.

Do you think that interest rates should be coming down?

VAN DREW: They should be coming down. They're too high. It's really clear. And you know what? Let's start taking them down. And if Powell is really right, then if there's a problem, you can always hold off on continuing to do that, or even raise them again.

I don't think that's the case. I think his policies have created a lack of growth in the United States that normally would occur. Now, it's time to do this. It's unfortunate that he won't.

You know, I wish that this administration and this President had the ability right now to choose a new Chairman of the Federal Reserve that is in sync with trying to make America better, stronger, faster, and, you know, develop economically more, but we are where we are, hopefully he will listen.

DEANGELIS: Congressman, great to see you tonight.

Congressman Jeff Van Drew, thank you.

END

data.title: Malaysia Shares Rise 1.1%; Volatility Could PersistMarket Talk
data.description: Malaysia's benchmark Kuala Lumpur Composite Index ended 1.1% higher at 1499.40 amid bargain hunting. The KLCI may stay volatile in the near term amid U.S.-China tensions, recession fears and Fed uncertainty, Hong Leong IB's Ng Jun Sheng says in a note. Still, downside risks are limited, with support at 1400-1450 likely to spur bargain hunting, the head of retail research says. Undemanding valuations and low foreign shareholding may offer a cushion, while Chinese President Xi Jinping's visit to Malaysia could boost sentiment and bilateral ties, he adds. Among gainers, Petronas Gas rose 2.8% and Dialog Group added 4.0%. Meanwhile, Nestle (Malaysia) was 2.2% lower and Axiata Group lost 0.5%. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Russian rouble strengthens past 81 vs U.S. dollar, up 40% since start of 2025
data.description:
The Russian rouble strengthened past 81 to the U.S. dollar, supported by rising oil prices, reaching its highest level since June 28, 2024.

At 0900 GMT the rouble was up 1.5% 80.90 to the U.S. currency in thin trade ahead of the Orthodox Easter holiday. The price for oil, Russia's main commodity, rose by 3% on April 17.

The Russian currency is up by over 40% against the dollar since the start of the year, mainly on expectation of easing of tensions between Russia and the United States.

The rouble has ignored the statement by U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio, that President Donald Trump will walk away from trying to broker a Russia-Ukraine peace deal within days unless there are clear signs that a deal can be done.

Against the Chinese yuan, which is used by Russia's central bank for its foreign exchange interventions and is currently the most traded foreign currency in Russia, the rouble was up 0.1% to 11.08 on the Moscow Stock Exchange.

data.title: Beijing Shougang 2024 Profit Down 29%, Revenue Slips 5%
data.description:
Beijing Shougang posted 2024 net profit attributable to shareholders of 471.1 million yuan, down 29% from 663.8 million yuan the previous year.

Earnings per share climbed to 0.0607 yuan from 0.0856 yuan, according to a Friday filing with the Shenzhen bourse.

The steel manufacturer's revenue declined 4.8% year over year to 108.3 billion yuan from 113.8 billion yuan.

Beijing Shougang declared a dividend of 0.022 yuan per share.

Shares of the company closed 4% higher Friday.

data.title: FocalTech Systems Repurchases Shares Worth NT$194 Million
data.description:
FocalTech Systems bought back 3 million shares worth NT$194 million under the repurchase program, according to a Thursday Taiwan Exchange filing.

The semiconductor company repurchased the shares at an average price of NT$64.67 per share.

data.title: AIPTEK International Board OKs Private Placement of Shares
data.description:
AIPTEK International's board approved a private placement of up to 25 million preferred shares, according to a Thursday Taiwan Exchange filing.

The shares will be issued to strategic investors, including major corporate directors, to strengthen its financial structure.

Proceeds will support the consumer electronic products company's long-term growth and capital needs.

data.title: Ukraine says aiming to wrap up mineral deal talks within a week
data.description:
Key points:
  • Deal will give USA privileged access to Ukraine's resources
  • Kyiv, Washington sign memorandum of intent
  • Ukraine seeking to fix ties after Oval Office shouting match

Kyiv said on Friday it aims to complete talks by the end of next week about a deal with Washington on jointly exploiting Ukrainian mineral resources, an agreement Ukrainian officials hope will go some way to firming up softening U.S. support for them in their war with Russia.

Though the terms of the eventual deal are not finalised, Washington has signalled it expects to have privileged access to Ukraine's natural resources by way of repayment for U.S. military support for the country over the past three years.

Late on Thursday, the U.S. and Ukrainian governments signed a memorandum of intent, signalling their intent to finalise the minerals deal.

The memorandum represented a step towards repairing ties between Kyiv and Washington that appeared to be hanging by a thread in February, when an Oval Office meeting between U.S. President Donald Trump and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy turned into a shouting match.

According to the text of the memorandum, published by the Ukrainian government on Friday, the aim of both sides is to complete discussions on the final deal by April 26, and to sign it soon after.

The memorandum said Ukrainian Prime Minister Denys Shmyhal will travel to Washington at the start of next week to meet U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and work on the deal together.

"We are happy to announce the signing with our American partners," Yulia Svyrydenko, Ukraine's first deputy prime minister and economy minister, said on social media on Thursday, referring to the memorandum of intent.

She said the document "testifies to the constructive joint work of our teams and the intention to finalise and conclude an agreement that will be beneficial to both our peoples."

According to the text of the memorandum, it paves the way for an economic partnership deal and the setting up of an investment fund for the reconstruction of Ukraine.

The text did not give any details about the terms of the eventual deal, including what access the United States would have, and how much revenue it would stand to gain.

In Washington, Trump told reporters: "We have a minerals deal, which I guess is going to be signed on Thursday."

Trump has been critical of the billions of dollars of aid that his predecessor, Joe Biden, gave to Ukraine, saying it was a bad deal for the United States. He has also indicated he wants closer ties with Moscow, which launched a full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

The White House did not respond to a request for further details on the timing and contents of the agreement.

PRIZED RESOURCES

A draft of the minerals deal that was under discussion earlier this month would give the U.S. privileged access to Ukraine's mineral deposits and require Kyiv to place in a joint investment fund all income from the exploitation of natural resources by Ukrainian state and private firms, according to a source with knowledge of the matter.

The draft deal included mineral deposits in Ukraine and also infrastructure for the transit of natural gas, the source said.

The proposed deal, however, would not provide U.S. security guarantees to Ukraine - a top priority of Kyiv's - for its fight against Russian forces occupying some 20% of its territory.

Inna Sovsun, a member of Ukraine's parliament, said she was grateful to Washington for the aid it has given, but said of a proposed minerals deal: “I do think that for us as Ukrainians, it feels a little bit like another country is using our vulnerability, which was not created by us.”

"It's also critically important when we are designing the future to keep in mind that people will live here in the future,” she told Reuters, speaking before the memorandum was signed.

Ukraine has sizable deposits of natural resources, including rare earths that are prized for use in electronics. It has deposits of graphite, lithium, titanium and uranium, among other minerals.

The path to a deal on minerals has been bumpy. A previous iteration of the deal was ready for signing in February, when Zelenskiy came to the White House to meet Trump.

That though was shelved after their meeting turned acrimonious - and Washington also briefly paused intelligence sharing with Ukraine, which is vital in its effort to resist the Russian invasion.

Later, Kyiv launched an effort to salvage the relationship, and began talking to U.S. officials again about cooperation on natural resources.

data.title: Japan futures fall for fifth straight week on China-US trade war fears
data.description:
• Japanese rubber futures fell on Friday, marking a fifth consecutive weekly loss, pressured by concerns over the China-U.S. trade war and a stronger yen, though trading was thin owing to Easter holidays.

  • The Osaka Exchange (OSE) rubber contract for September delivery , finished down 1.2 yen, or 0.41%, at 290.9 yen ($2) per kg.

  • The contract lost 2.3% over the week.

  • The rubber contract on the Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE) for September delivery slid 65 yuan to finish at 14,590 yuan ($1,998) a metric ton.

  • "Concerns over slowing global demand amid the tariff war have dampened market sentiment," said Yutaka Trusty Securities analyst Jiong Gu.

  • "However, given the low rubber inventory levels in Japan and relatively cheaper domestic prices, the market may be near its bottom and could rebound next week, led by near-term contracts," he said.

  • U.S. President Donald Trump on Thursday signalled a potential end to the tit-for-tat tariff increases between the U.S. and China that shocked markets and said that a deal over the fate of social media platform TikTok may have to wait.

  • Though the U.S. and China are in touch, sources told Reuters that free-flowing, high-level exchanges that would lead to a deal have largely been absent.

  • The yen traded around 142.32 against the U.S. dollar, compared with 142.72 yen in late Thursday trade in Asia.

  • A stronger currency makes yen-denominated assets less affordable for overseas buyers.

  • Japan's Nikkei share average rose 1% on Friday to clock its best week in three months as investors turned more hopeful that Trump would be able to broker trade deals with some of its top trading partners, including Japan.

  • Singapore's financial markets were closed on Friday for a public holiday. Trading will resume on Monday, April 21.

($1 = 142.3800 yen)

($1 = 7.3015 Chinese yuan renminbi)

data.title: On-Chain Experiment Or Rug Pull? Base Faces Backlash After Unofficial Memecoin Crashes 90%
data.description:
Coinbase’s Layer-2 (L2) Network, Base, has faced intense backlash over rug-pull allegations after it promoted an unofficial memecoin that crashed by over 90%, sparking a debate about the future of memecoins and on-chain content.

The Rise And Fall Of Base’s Unofficial Memecoin

On Wednesday, Base’s official X account posted an image with the text “Base is for everyone.” Moments later, they shared a link to the on-chain social protocol Zora and the caption “Coined it,” sparking a speculative frenzy among investors.

The protocol allows users to make social media posts into tradable tokens, automatically minting them. After Base’s post was turned into a token, the crypto community quickly skyrocketed its market capitalization to $17 million.

However, online reports showed that the memecoin collapsed by around 92% after the top holders, who owned 47% of the supply, sold the memecoin just over an hour after launching.

Some community members noted that the token was “HORRIFICALLY sniped,” while on-chain data analytics platform Lookonchain highlighted that “3 wallets bought a large amount of ‘Base is for everyone’ before Base posted and sold them, making a profit of ~$666K.”

As a result, the community criticized the network’s team for the memecoin, calling the incident a rug pull and asking them to “stop launching worthless tokens that will all inevitably go to 0. You are diluting your brand and the value of real base assets.”

Zora data shows Base has earned around $81,000 from the memecoin, which has recovered from the initial sell-off with a peak market capitalization of $26 million before retracing to the $9 million-$10 million range.

Base’s Public Experiment

Base responded to the backlash, clarifying they will never sell their holdings, but they weren’t an official network token either. The team explained that they posted on Zora because they believe everyone should bring content on-chain and use the tools that make it possible.

Memes. Moments. Culture. If we want the future to be onchain, we have to be willing to experiment in public. That’s what we’re doing. To be clear, Base will never sell these tokens, and ​​these are not official network tokens for Base, Coinbase, or any other related product. The content we share is creative, and we’re going to keep bringing culture onchain.

The public on-chain experimentation opened a debate about memecoin culture and on-chain content, with Base’s creator, Jesse Pollak, weighing in.

In a series of X posts, Pollak explained that “not all coins are the same,” outlining the differences between these two types of tokens. Is On-Chain Content The Future For Creators?

According to his posts, a contentcoin is one piece of content with singular value and no expectations. Additionally, multiple of them can be created by the same person, with “big ones” potentially turning into memes.

On the contrary, a memecoin is an “aggregation of content,” with aggregated value and high expectations, where the creator “should” only create one. He also noted that big ones turn into projects.

Pollack considers that “someone has to normalize putting all of our content onchain. and i’m not afraid for it to be us. why? because in the wake of the chaos, we’ll normalize the behavior and create a better future for creators.”

Nonetheless, many users remain skeptical, with community members also criticizing Base’s post announcing investors can mint a deleted scene of the “Vitalik: An Ethereum Story” documentary, where the project’s founder, Vitalik Buterin, shows what’s in his backpack.

“Through ‘the financialization of everything’ we come to learn that most things are worthless,” the user stated.

data.title: Nisso to Acquire Man to Man Holdings in Strategic Expansion Move
data.description:
Human resources company Nisso is acquiring Man to Man Holdings through a share purchase and simplified share exchange, for about 1.6 billion yen, making it a wholly owned subsidiary by June, according to its Thursday bourse filing.

The acquisition aims to strengthen Nisso's presence in the Chubu-Tokai region, Japan, particularly in manufacturing-related human resources services for automotive and semiconductor industries.

The transaction is pending regulatory approval and is expected to enhance Nisso's competitive edge while contributing to the growth of Japan's manufacturing sector.

data.title: Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) contract prices ease in Europe
data.description: This week, PVC contract prices decreased, while spot prices quoted flat in the European region.An industry source in Europe informed a Polymerupdate team member, "European spot prices for polyvinyl chloride remained stable over the past week, as sluggish demand eased some of the supply constraints. The European PVC market was predominantly influenced by various regional production challenges, alongside a rise in imports of Asian suspension PVC (s-PVC). Asian spot shipments continue to be available at attractive prices. Traders noted that the arbitrage opportunity for importing from Asia to Europe is still viable. However, consumers have shown increased reluctance to place orders for Asian products due to the decline in European naphtha prices observed in April. Naphtha is a crucial feedstock for ethylene, which is essential for PVC production. In recent weeks, supply in Europe has tightened. A regional manufacturer has declared force majeure on the production of suspension polyvinyl chloride for K70 and K64 grades, with the disruption anticipated to last several weeks at its facility in Tavaux, France. This follows another producer's declaration of force majeure concerning PVC and vinyl chloride monomer at its Marl site in Germany. Additionally, a PVC production unit in Poland faced renewed technical difficulties, leading to the cancellation of April deliveries after previous issues in March. Maintenance work at the PVC production site in Pernis, the Netherlands, has also been extended. Despite the force majeure declarations resulting in reduced supply, the overall situation is not critically tight, as low purchasing interest has mitigated some of the supply-side pressures. Buyer responses have been varied, with many noting the relative ease of finding alternative sources to compensate for the lost supply.The source added,

data.description: On Thursday, paraxylene prices climbed in the European region.An industry source in Europe, wishing to remain unidentified, informed a Polymerupdate team member,

data.title: U.S. House Committee Urges Two U.S. Banks to Withdraw From CATL's Listing2nd Update
data.description:
By Sherry Qin

A U.S. congressional committee has urged two U.S. banks to pull out of working on Chinese battery giant Contemporary Amperex Technology's planned listing in Hong Kong, expressing "significant concerns" over their involvement in the process.

By underwriting CATL's initial public offering, Bank of America and JPMorgan Chase are exposing themselves to "serious regulatory, financial, and reputational risks," Rep. John Moolenaar (R., Mich.), chairman of the House Select Committee on the Chinese Communist Party, wrote in letters addressed to the lenders' chief executives on Thursday.

The letters to BofA CEO Brian Moynihan and JPMorgan's Jamie Dimon made reference to President Trump's "America First Investment Policy," aimed at preventing U.S. investments into companies allegedly tied to China's military.

"CATL's IPO is the exact kind of investment this policy was designed to block," wrote Moolenaar, head of the committee that describes itself as committed to building bipartisan consensus on the threat posed by the Chinese Communist Party.

The Pentagon in January added CATL and other well-known Chinese businesses to a list of companies it identifies as military in nature. The battery maker protested the move at the time and a spokesperson reiterated on Friday that the designation of CATL as a Chinese military company is a mistake.

"CATL has never engaged in any military-related business or activities," the company spokesperson said.

The allegations in the letter by Representative Moolenaar are factually incorrect and without merit, the spokesperson said. CATL's Hong Kong listing is progressing as planned and it is engaging with the U.S. Department of Defense to clarify any misunderstandings, the spokesperson added.

Bank of America and JPMorgan Chase declined to comment.

The electric-vehicle battery maker, which is listed in Shenzhen, applied for a secondary listing in Hong Kong earlier this year to advance its global expansion strategy and improve its competitiveness.

Analysts say CATL could raise around $5 billion in the offering, which would make it the city's largest listing since ByteDance rival Kuaishou's $5.4 billion IPO in 2021.

CATL, which supplies batteries to automakers such as Tesla and Volkswagen, accounted for more than one-third of the global battery market in 2024, more than double that of BYD, which ranked second, according to Seoul-based SNE Research.

Shares of CATL ended 0.2% lower on Friday.

Write to Sherry Qin at sherry.qin@wsj.com

data.title: Nippon Reit Investment Issues 1.3 Billion Yen in Green Bonds to Fund Asset Acquisition
data.description:
Nippon Reit Investment is issuing 1.3 billion yen in unsecured green bonds, maturing in April 2030, to fund the acquisition of two Tokyo properties, according to its Thursday bourse filing.

The bonds, carrying a 1.346% annual interest rate, will be issued on April 23, 2025, with biannual interest payments.

The issuance earned the highest Green1(F) rating from Japan Credit Rating Agency for its Green Finance Framework.

Major underwriters include Mitsubishi UFJ Morgan Stanley Securities and SMBC Nikko Securities, with commitments from regional banks like The Sugamo Shinkin Bank.
